name: Improve hunk rendering and scroll performance

description: Baseline: add representative render/scroll fps benchmark plus correctness checks for split/stack scrolling and hunk navigation
Memoize diff sections and row rendering so hunk navigation only rerenders the active file and changed rows
Try memoized row bodies so selected-line markers can update without rebuilding split and stack cell content
Inline split and stack cell spans to reduce renderer node count while keeping diff formatting unchanged
Memoize App-level derived lists and totals to avoid recomputing filtered files and hunk cursors on every navigation step
Skip the extra wrapper box for note-free diff rows so steady-state scrolling traverses a smaller tree
Fold the hunk-selection marker into split and stack cell text to remove another per-row box
Inline the split separator inside the right cell so split rows render with one less box per line
Render split and stack lines as single text rows so marker, gutters, separator, and content share one row box
Inline the selected marker in header and collapsed rows to remove another hot-path wrapper box
Use a cheaper single-box path for non-annotated header rows while preserving the badge path for annotated hunks
Cache highlighted diffs per file so revisiting files avoids repeat highlighting work
Reuse cached highlighted diffs during render so revisiting a file avoids the effect/state round-trip
Remove redundant layout props from memoized row views because row types already encode split versus stack
Try memoizing whole hunk groups so navigation compares fewer top-level items instead of every row
Compute trimmed span width in the same pass so line padding no longer needs a second reduce
Try a leaner span-trimming loop that avoids object spread and .at() in the hot render path
Try caching repeated space strings for gutter blanks and padding in the line render hot path
Try caching built diff rows per file and layout so revisiting files can reuse normalized row models
Try removing pre-update scrollChildIntoView calls and rely solely on the selection-followup effect
